ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for aloo saag with basmati rice; mapped to closest parent 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03315091). Aloo saag component lacks a dedicated FoodOn URI.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dishes — mixed rice dish with vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (mixed rice dish with vegetables) used as closest parent analogue for aloo saag with basmati rice. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific Indian-origin dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for aloo saag with basmati rice as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of aloo saag with basmati rice is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of basmati rice and 150 g of aloo saag curry, as typically served in a Singapore hawker or Indian restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Saag Free Basmati Rice — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database record for 'Aloo Saag Free Basmati Rice' (cr_id G00010525) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; estimated at 0.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable potato-spinach curry with oil (USDA SR Legacy #11457 spinach cooked and #11352 potato boiled, proportional blend), documented as adapted. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; Basmati rice GI ~58; potato boiled GI ~78; blended dish estimate weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aloo saag with basmati rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 using a carbohydrate-weighted blend: basmati rice (GI 58, ~60% of dish carbohydrate contribution) and boiled potato (GI 78, ~40% of dish carbohydrate contribution), yielding weighted GI = (58×0.60)+(78×0.40) = 34.8+31.2 = 66, adjusted downward to 56 to account for the fibre buffering effect of spinach (3.7 g/100g dietary fibre) and the fat content (4.21 g/100g) which slows gastric emptying. GI source: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 75.57) / 100 = 42.3 (carbohydrate per 300g serving = 25.19 × 3 = 75.57g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; net sodium load at full serving (300g) is 693 mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as the 693 mg sodium per standard 300 g serving approaches 30% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, despite the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio of 1.41 which provides partial DASH-diet benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Dual starch load from potato (GI ~78) and basmati rice (GI ~58) produces a high glycaemic load of 42.3 per standard 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or substantially reduce this dish due to the high glycaemic load of 42.3 per serving driven by the dual starch combination of potato and basmati rice; substituting cauliflower rice for basmati rice and reducing potato quantity can lower the glycaemic load to an acceptable range, and post-meal glucose monitoring is strongly advised if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol-free with 4.21 g/100g total fat; sodium at 231 mg/100g and estimated saturated fat at 0.8 g/100g are the primary cardiovascular considerations
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is cholesterol-free and relatively low in saturated fat (~0.8 g/100g), making it acceptable for cardiovascular disease patients in moderate portions (150–200 g), provided salt is reduced during preparation and cooking oil is minimised; the iron (2.55 mg/100g), potassium (327 mg/100g), and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) from spinach and potato provide positive cardiovascular micronutrient contributions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Multiple nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, green chili) contain solanine which may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should avoid this dish or request a nightshade-free preparation substituting potato with sweet potato or chickpeas and omitting tomato and green chili, while retaining turmeric and ginger for their anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Saag with Basmati Rice is a North Indian-origin vegetarian dish widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker centres. 'Aloo' means potato in Hindi and Urdu, while 'saag' refers to leafy greens — most commonly spinach (palak) in this preparation. The dish consists of diced or cubed potatoes cooked with fresh spinach in a spiced tomato-onion gravy seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, and green chili, served alongside steamed basmati rice. The texture is semi-dry to semi-gravy, with soft potato pieces and wilted spinach coated in a fragrant, mildly spiced sauce. The flavour profile is earthy, mildly spiced, and savoury with a slight bitterness from the spinach balanced by the starchiness of potato and the floral aroma of basmati rice. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100g with 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrates. It is notably high in dietary fibre (3.7g/100g), iron (2.55mg/100g), potassium (327mg/100g), and calcium (54mg/100g), largely attributable to the spinach content. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Clinically, the dual starch load from potato and basmati rice creates a medium-to-high glycaemic index (~56) and a high glycaemic load per standard serving (~42.3 for 300g), making it unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients without significant modification. The sodium content of 231mg/100g requires monitoring for hypertension patients. The presence of nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, green chili) is a consideration for rheumatoid arthritis patients with solanine sensitivity. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it broadly accessible across Singapore's multicultural population.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010525) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is a primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and documented as adapted. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr) using a carbohydrate-weighted blend of basmati rice and boiled potato GI values, adjusted for fibre and fat buffering.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable spinach-potato curry preparations, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data is not available in the HPB Foodo source or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields require laboratory analysis or a more detailed composite food database entry to populate. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero consistent with the all-plant ingredient composition of this dish. 4. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for aloo saag with basmati rice as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 5. The dish name in the input contains a heart emoji ('❤️ Free') which appears to be a menu label indicating a heart-healthy or reduced-fat variant; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Aloo Saag with Basmati Rice'. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest parent analogue; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific dish. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315091 mapped as closest parent class 'rice dish'; no exact FoodOn term for aloo saag with basmati rice exists. 8. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodon_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.

Updated notes to document this correction. All other sections preserved from iteration 2.
